title: Geometric memory in incomplete phase transitions across dimensions
abstract: We model a direct solid-state phase transition through a nucleation-and-growth process in which plates have simple, regular shapes - squares, cubes, or square-faced lamellae - and grow homothetically (self-similarly) until they either reach a randomly assigned maximum size or are stopped by impingement with previously formed plates. The reverse transformation is represented by the preferential disappearance of smaller plates, while larger plates are retained during an incomplete reversion. A subsequent direct transformation therefore produces a modified plate-size distribution, a memory effect that forms the main focus of this study. Building upon an earlier two-dimensional (2D) formulation, we extend the model to cubes (3D) and to lamellar plates (3DL) in order to examine how dimensionality affects transformation memory. We introduce a quantitative descriptor of memory, the size mass ratio, and find that memory is robust in all geometries but overall stronger in 2D than in 3D or 3DL. We provide growth snapshots, arrest-regrowth cycles, size distributions, and differential scanning calorimetry simulations, and we compute the Shannon size-entropy to quantify configurational diversity. Although motivated by the thermal memory effect in shape-memory alloys, the model more generally identifies a purely geometric mechanism for memory in first-order solid-solid transformations, highlighting the role of dimensionality and geometric blocking in controlling the strength of transformation memory.
summary: This paper demonstrates that memory effects in phase transitions can emerge purely from geometric constraints like nucleation, growth, and impingement without needing explicit thermodynamics. It shows that the strength of this 'geometric memory' is highly dependent on the dimensionality of the system, being most pronounced in 2D.
main_problem: Investigating the mechanism of geometric memory in incomplete first-order solid-state phase transitions and how dimensionality (2D, 3D, and 3D lamellar) affects the strength of this memory effect.
main_result: The study finds that memory is robust across all geometries but is strongest in 2D and weakest in 3D lamellar structures, driven purely by geometric constraints like growth and impingement.
method: A discrete lattice-based stochastic nucleation-and-growth model using brute-force seeding and shape-preserving dilation algorithms.
model_or_system: A minimal geometric model of phase transformations involving the nucleation and self-similar growth of plates (2D squares), cubes (3D), and lamellae (3D with fixed thickness) that are arrested by impingement or intrinsic size limits.
key_observables: Size Mass Ratio (SMR), Shannon size-entropy, plate-size distributions, and simulated Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) signals.
important_parameters: Minimum and maximum plate sizes (Lmin, Lmax), domain size (A), lamellar thickness (Tw), and the arrest size (s).
main_assumptions: The model excludes explicit energetic, elastic, or thermodynamic terms, relying solely on geometric blocking; it also assumes the reverse transformation proceeds via the preferential disappearance of smaller plates.
figures_summary: Snapshots of growth progression and arrest-regrowth cycles; mass-per-size histograms showing depletion at arrested sizes; and simulated DSC signals showing characteristic dips or shoulders.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the geometric memory concept, describes the nucleation-and-growth model, presents results for 2D, 3D, and 3DL geometries, quantifies memory via SMR and entropy, and discusses the implications of dimensionality and the 'hammering' effect.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. 2D squares. Snapshots at (a) low coverage, (b) moderate coverage, and (c) the jamming limit. Visualization run for A = 30. Each new square is randomly placed among available L2 min positions. Lmin = 3 while Lmax = 7 and the maximum intrinsic size L for each new square is randomly chosen in this interval. The progressive crowding reduces available perimeter for growth and only small squares can grow towards jamming.

Fig. 1 illustrates the gradual filling of a large square domain (side A = 30) with smaller squares whose sizes range from Lmin = 3 to Lmax = 7. In our model, each newly placed square nucleates at the minimum size and then “grows” to a target size drawn randomly from the interval (Lmin, Lmax), while preserving its square shape. Owing to this randomness in size selection, the early stages of the transformation contain comparable numbers of squares of all sizes. Fig. 1a displays a single random placement of the first few squares, but when averaged over many realizations the number of squares per size tends to equalize. Panels (b) and (c) make visually clear how, as space fills, new squares are...

FIG. 2. 3D cubes. Snapshots at (a) low coverage, (b) moderate coverage, and (c) the jamming limit. Visualization run for A = 30. Brute-force seeding uniformly among available L3 min places, then growth up to a random maximum intrinsic size, not bigger than Lmax = 7. In three dimensions, accessible free surface collapses faster than in 2D, favoring earlier lack of space for larger cubes.

FIG. 3. 3DL lamellar plates. (a) Three plates nucleate randomly and expand homothetically to their intrinsic sizes in the YZ, XZ, and XY planes. (b) As space becomes crowded, geometric constraints may prevent full growth, so plates 4 and 5 remain smaller due to impingement.

FIG. 4. 3DL lamellar plates. Snapshots at (a) low coverage, (b) moderate coverage, and (c) the jamming limit. Visualization run for A = 30. Perfect-square plates in XY/XZ/YZ planes with fixed thickness TW = 2 along the normal. Brute-force seeding among available L3 min seeds; Lmin = 3, Lmax = 7. Lateral impingement dominates and freezes many nuclei early, yielding a “bottom-heavy” size distribution.
